pages.title: How McDonald’s Canada is reshaping a seasonal offering into a summer ritual » Strategy
pages.content:
Home
»
Campaign
,
QSR
»
How McDonald’s Canada is reshaping a seasonal offering into a summer ritual
By Laurie Wilson
May 30, 2025
Over the years, McDonald’s branding approach for Summer Drink Days and Summer Treat Days has evolved from sales promotion into seasonal ritual. The latest iteration leans into the idea harder than ever.
A new campaign with creative and strategy led by Cossette pushes deal appeal to the background in favour of emotional context as the QSR positions the brand event as an unofficial kickoff of summer.
Rebecca Smart, marketing director of brand strategy at McDonald’s Canada, tells
strategy
that recurring characters and slice-of-life moments serve as brand-identity counterpoints to the price-and-product origins of the promotions.
“We need to be sure we’re leveraging lower-funnel tactics to drive direct sales activation, but we can’t forget the huge equity in Summer Drink Days and Summer Treat Days as an annual brand event,” Smart say. “So with this year’s campaigns, we’ve been equally focused on continuing to build that sustained top-of-mind awareness and make sure that whenever Canadians think ‘summer drinks’ or ‘summer treats,’ they think of McDonald’s.”
Smart says social-media insights from years past that showed anticipation building as early as March and April informed the strategy to position the events as a cultural start to summer. Summer Drink Days is now in its 16th season and Summer Treat Days is entering its eighth.
“We know there’s a limited amount of time we have as Canadians to enjoy the summer weather and, more important than ever, we need to also give our guests a great reason for them to choose us as a destination,” she says.
Cossette developed a cast of characters that includes a senior duo showcasing their swimming swagger and a casually observing pool boy who appears in both 30-second hero spots.
Agency executive creative director Andrew Chisholm says the creative goal was “to personify the brand’s connection to spontaneous fun, nostalgia, and the shared anticipation of warm weather tradition.”
“By using humour with heart and a consistent narrative voice, anchored by our recurring guide, we were able to reflect the spirit of Canadian summers while reinforcing McDonald’s as part of the seasonal ritual,” Chisholm says. “The result feels both memorable and unmistakably on-brand.”
OMD worked on media for the campaign that launched last week and is scheduled to run through Sept. 1 on multiple touchpoints including TV, digital video, audio, OOH and social media.
Tags:
Andrew Chisholm
,
cossette
,
McDonald's

pages.title: McDonald’s Marketing Strategy: Why It’s Still the King of Fast Food in 2026
pages.content:
blog
Food & Beverage Marketing
McDonald’s Marketing Strategy: Why It’s Still the King of Fast Food in 2026
Whether you’re grabbing breakfast in Tokyo or a late-night meal in Paris, McDonald’s feels instantly familiar.
McDonald’s marketing strategy is built on consistency, emotional relevance, and the ability to feel local in every market.
Familiarity carries through in its marketing by showing how different generations, cultures, and communities connect through a shared, yet locally adapted McDonald’s experience.
It’s global, but it still feels personal.
In this post, I’ll break down how McDonald’s achieves that,
step by step.
If you are in a rush, please use the links below to jump to the section you need.
McDonald’s Marketing Mix (But in 7Ps)
Who Actually Is McDonald’s Target Audience?
McDonald’s Brand Positioning
McDonald’s Brand Loyalty
McDonald’s Social Media Strategy (Powered by Memes and MilkShakes)
McDonald’s versus Burger King Marketing Strategy
McDonald’s Advertising: Culture-Driven, Visual, and Adaptable
What to Remember from McDonald’s Marketing
If you are ready to dig in, let’s start from the very beginning.
Back in
1940
, brothers Richard and Maurice McDonald opened the first McDonald’s restaurant in San Bernardino, California. In 1948, they revolutionized the business with the introduction of the “Speedee Service System,” setting the template for modern fast food.
As of 2026, McDonald’s operates over
38,000 restaurants
across more than 100 countries. Systemwide sales, which include total sales across all company-operated and franchised restaurants, surpassed
$130 billion
for the year.
However, McDonald’s didn’t achieve rapid growth and its current position through hard work alone, but also by implementing its well-built
marketing strategies for fast food restaurants
.
When the company was searching for new ways to catch the attention of customers in the 1970s, they launched the Happy Meal, one of their most outstanding achievements.
This led to the world’s first
Happy Meal
, a combination of meals suitable for children, including a free toy.
After that, McDonald’s momentum never really slowed.
Franchising
took off, the
Drive-Thru
made fast food even faster, and menu icons like the
Big Mac
and
McMuffin
turned into global staples. With each decade, the brand found new ways to connect—rolling out
McCafé,
embracing
digital orders
, and constantly refreshing the menu to fit local tastes.
McDonald’s Kept Evolving—What’s Yours?
We’ve reviewed countless case studies and handpicked two agencies that truly stand out in
food marketing.
Each brings creativity, strategy, and flavor to brands that know how to connect with hungry audiences, like McDonald’s does.
Favoured
We chose
Favoured
for its standout results in food marketing—growing
The Fat Crab’s
organic reach by
104.9%
and increasing engagement for
Putt Putt Noodle
by
20%
.
Visit Website
eDesign Interactive
The agency is worth noting for its blend of creativity and tech. Their
Welch’s Fruit Snacks
website bursts with fun, while their work for
Hardie’s
uses
artificial neural networks
to deliver a digital experience.
Visit Website
Breaking Down McDonald’s Marketing Strategy: A Careful Analysis
How can you serve billions of customers each year?
McDonald’s marketing strategy focuses on appealing to a wide range of customers, from young children to families, and even to health-conscious consumers (with their catchy slogans, such as “
I’m Lovin’ It
” and “
Have It Your Way.
“)
The magic of McDonald’s is in the details. It makes global feel familiar, and local feel special, no matter where you order.
McDonald’s offers local menu staples, McAloo Tikki in India and SamuraiMac in Japan:
Localization of the brand and the products is a key move in McDonald’s marketing; local offerings only generate
30% of the overall sales,
while the remaining
70% is driven by its core global classics
.
Image source:
WSJ
Localization goes beyond just the menu. McDonald’s adapts its restaurant designs to reflect local lifestyles—French locations, for example, are known for their chic, café-inspired interiors (as seen in
Emily in Paris
).
Let’s break down its every aspect from marketing mix and branding to social media and ads.
McDonald’s Marketing Mix (But in 7PS)
McDonald’s marketing mix shows how a global brand can scale while staying agile and locally relevant. In this section, we’ll reference an article by
Claudio Vignali
, a lecturer at Manchester Metropolitan University, titled “McDonald’s: ‘Think Global, Act Local’ – The Marketing Mix.”
1.Product: Streamlined Offerings with Cultural Sensitivity
Rather than offering a one-size-fits-all menu, McDonald’s adapts its product lineup to fit
cultural and regional preferences
. As the article notes,
in India, McDonald’s removed beef, mutton, and pork items
from its menu—an adaptation made to align with religious and cultural values.
This strategic localization reflects the company’s broader philosophy:
global branding with regional flexibility
.
By limiting the product range on purpose, McDonald’s not only simplifies operations but also
enhances its alignment with local tastes
, helping the brand remain relevant across diverse markets.
2.Price: Adaptive Value Strategies
McDonald’s focus on pricing reflects the market’s economic environment. Rather than applying a fixed global pricing model,
McDonald’s adjusts prices
according to local conditions—an approach that positions the brand as both affordable and accessible.
The decision to
limit the menu
also supports
pricing efficiency
, reducing overhead while maintaining profitability. This cost-control mechanism allows McDonald’s to offer value without compromising quality, particularly in markets where price sensitivity is high.
3.Place: Proximity as a Power Move
McDonald’s location strategy relies on a franchising model that allows rapid expansion while maintaining brand consistency. New restaurants are placed based on data—foot traffic, surrounding businesses, population density, and accessibility all play a role in determining where to grow next.
Typical locations include:
Urban centers
Transit hubs
Busy shopping districts
Each site is chosen to align with customer routines. The goal is simple: be where people already spend time, and make stopping by easy. Local franchisees bring regional knowledge to the table, helping the brand adapt to community habits while upholding global standards.
This approach keeps McDonald’s highly visible, easy to reach, and deeply
embedded in daily life
.
4.Promotion: Blending Tangibility with Emotion
McDonald’s success in promotion is rooted in its ability to deliver both
tangible elements
(such as consistent packaging and product presentation) and
intangible elements
(like emotional connection and familiarity). These combine to form a brand experience that feels consistent globally but still resonates on a local level.
McDonald’s integrates
localization
into its promotional strategy—adapting messaging, visuals, and even in-store design to fit regional consumer behavior and preferences. This approach supports its “
think global, act local
” mantra.
5.People: Service Standardization with Local Understanding
At the heart of McDonald’s operations is a
consistent service culture
upheld by strict staff training and performance guidelines.
Regardless of location, customers can expect the same level of
friendliness, cleanliness, and efficiency
. Employees are trained to embody the brand’s core values, ensuring a uniform customer experience worldwide.
At the same time, staffing is localized. Hiring from the local population allows each restaurant to better connect with community expectations, cultural norms, and service styles. This balance between
global standards and local familiarity
helps build trust and repeat visits in diverse markets.
6.Process: Streamlined Operations that Scale
McDonald’s operational model is engineered for speed, simplicity, and consistency.
Processes are tightly structured, from food preparation to delivery, ordering systems to in-store workflow, making it easy to replicate success across thousands of outlets.
The result is a
reliable customer experience
, whether in a bustling urban drive-thru or a suburban dine-in.
These standardized systems:
Reduce wait times
Enhance staff productivity
Reinforce the brand promise
7.Physical Evidence: Branded Consistency Across Touchpoints
Every detail of the McDonald’s environment is designed to reinforce its identity and create a familiar, reliable experience. Key elements include:
Architecture:
Consistent exterior design with signature elements like the Golden Arches
Interior layout:
Functional, standardized restaurant flow for dine-in and takeaway
Staff uniforms:
Branded attire that reflects cleanliness, professionalism, and brand identity
Menu boards:
Clear, visually unified digital or printed displays across locations
Packaging:
Iconic wrappers, cups, and bags that are both functional and recognizable
Dining area cleanliness:
Maintained to meet global hygiene standards
Signage clarity:
Easy-to-read visuals that guide customers efficiently
Navigation ease:
Intuitive layout that supports a seamless customer journey
Who Actually Is McDonald’s Target Audience?
McDonald’s target audience is intentionally broad, covering a wide range of demographics and lifestyles. From children and families to students, professionals, and late-night customers, the brand is designed to be accessible to all income levels and age groups.
McDonald’s target market strategy focuses on being
familiar, inclusive, and emotionally resonant
. It builds its messaging around themes like
nostalgia
,
convenience
, and
shared experiences
, rather than targeting one narrow segment.
The
statement
in the FAQ section of McDonald’s official website testifies to what I said above:
McDonald’s aims to offer a friendly, fun environment for everyone, and we mean everyone, to enjoy. This means appealing to families who love our iconic Happy Meal®, to workers grabbing breakfast on-the-go or eating in to enjoy our freshly ground coffee and free WiFi.
By operating a variety of third-party data sources – demographic, psychographic, and behavioral data – to create audience segments that are relevant to its products & services. For instance, McDonald’s might target its ads for “Happy Meals” to families with young children or “Create Your Taste” to millennials while targeting health-conscious consumers with its menu options, such as salads and smoothies.
At that point, it is possible to say that data clicked to age, gender, location, income, household size, education level, values, lifestyle, app usage, and social media activity act as a source for the fast food chain to target its advertising and marketing campaigns in addition to personalizing its customer experience (CX).
Before closing that chapter, I would also like to mention a surprising fact about the typical McDonald’s customer. According to a highly prestigious source, Business Insider, the average customer of the brand is strikingly
similar to the Walmart customer
– here are the details:
The typical McDonald’s customer is a married white woman between the ages of 41 and 56, according to data provided by analytics firm Numerator. She likely lives in a suburban area in the southeastern US, and her household makes more than $80,000 a year. This typical shopper either has an undergraduate degree or attended some college.
After all these efforts… How satisfied the customers of McDonald’s are?
Before releasing the figures, I’d like to remind you what the American Customer Satisfaction Index is. In the most basic terms, ACSI is a benchmark that tracks customer satisfaction across various industries in the US. ACSI scores range from 0 to 100, with higher scores indicating greater customer satisfaction.  While the ACSI score doesn’t provide a specific satisfaction percentage, a score above 70 is generally considered good.
According to a recent survey released by Statistica, McDonald’s ACSI score for US restaurants in 2023 was
69
, and this figure falls within the “
neutral
” satisfaction range, according to the ACSI website.
Additionally, the 2021-dated
Real Research Consumer Survey
found that:
38.49%
of respondents were satisfied with the food at McDonald’s.
23.68%
were neutral.
33.22%
disliked the burgers (though satisfaction with other menu items might be higher).
While not a negative score, it suggests there’s room for improvement in customer satisfaction for McDonald’s.
McDonald’s Brand Positioning
Brand awareness Oscar goes to McDonald’s, for sure.
McDonald’s has successfully marketed itself for decades based on core values of convenience, affordability, and fun.
The restaurant is well-known for providing fast and tasty food at an affordable price, making it a popular destination for families and friends worldwide. The brand’s emphasis on creating a welcoming atmosphere and maintaining high standards has contributed to its widespread recognition.
@mcdonalds
major perks of the job
#socialmediamanager
#mcd
♬ original sound – McDonald’s
In addition to offering play areas for kids and convenient takeout options, McDonald’s has also made impressive strides in the digital space, achieving impressive feats. Its digital marketing strategy is constantly evolving to keep up with the latest trends & technologies.
@mcdonalds
if u guess the color i’ll like your comment
♬ original sound – McDonald’s
Another great example that reminds customers of their childhood:
@mcdonalds
theyre gonna have to climb up the slide and get me
#mcdonalds
#horace
♬ Funk It Up – Official Sound Studio
The company is always looking for new ways to reach and engage its target audience online, by using a variety of online channels and tactics including SEM,
SEO strategies
, social media marketing, email marketing, and mobile marketing.
McDonald’s Brand Loyalty
McDonald’s isn’t just selling fries and burgers; they’re selling an emotional connection. In other words, the fast-food giant really understands that brand loyalty goes beyond just delicious fries and burgers. That’s why they’ve cultivated a following by creating a multi-faceted approach that fosters a strong connection with their customers.
When it comes to building brand loyalty, the cornerstone is McDonald’s rewarding programs. Through basic apps like The
My McDonald’s Rewards
program, the brand allows customers to earn points with purchases and redeem them for free food.
Similarly, their online stores like
Golden Arches Unlimited
are giving customers a way to express their love for the brand and creating a permanent place for them to engage with McDonald’s beyond just grabbing a meal.
This is actually a clever (and basic) loyalty program strategy; from both the sides of the customer and the brand itself.  By offering unique branded merchandise, McDonald’s taps into fans’ desire to celebrate their affinity with the brand.  This isn’t new –  McDonald’s has been partnering with fashion brands for merchandise since the 1980s, and many loyal customers likely remember the
McDelivery Collection
.
What’s more, Colin Mitchell, Senior VP of the brand’s Global Marketing commented in a statement:
McDonald’s has been ingrained in the fabric of culture for years, and there’s a long history of fans wearing our brand with pride. We’re excited to help customers wear their brand love on their sleeves with the unveiling of Golden Arches Unlimited as we continue to inspire feel-good moments with McDonald’s.
McDonald’s Social Media Strategy (Powered by Memes and MilkShakes)
The fast food giant has a bold online presence on all major social media platforms, which can be considered the core element of McDonald’s digital marketing strategy.
McDonald’s social media strategies are rooted in a blend of cultural relevance, brand consistency, and everyday engagement, showing just how vital creative
food marketing agencies
can be.
From platform-specific content and playful memes to
nostalgia
, pop culture moments, and user-generated campaigns,
McDonald’s meets people where they are, without ever losing its brand voice, with the help of
🕰️ Nostalgia
🌟 Pop culture moments
🤳 User-generated content (UGC)
😂 Memes
📲 Platform-specific content
Using Unique Artistry
The fact: McDonald’s uses unique artistry in each aspect of its digital marketing – creative visuals, storytelling, humor, and interactivity to connect with its target group on a deeper level.
As a great example of that approach, 4 years ago the brand teamed up with
TBWA/Paris
and allowed its iconic French fries to show the way.
These digital ads are inspired by minimalism, the emblematic artistic movement of the 21st century. According to the directions, 8 pop-colored posters are proposed to make 64 different combinations. The campaign was art directed by Emile Chiumino and Camille Roulant. With that campaign, while most brands put their regular logo and an arrow as a direction for their customers, McDonald’s has taken a more novel approach.
TBWA/Paris X McDonald’s collaboration continues with similar works:
Teaming up with Celebs
Yes, teaming up with celebrities is a common and effective way for any kind of brand to reach a wider audience and build excitement for their products or services; but, McDonald’s makes it in a better way.
In 2021, McDonald’s partnered with Cardi B to create a custom meal called the Cardi B & Offset Meal. Since that date, the well-known couple has promoted the menu on various channels.
For instance, the following TikTok video hit 24M views and over 3M likes.
@mcdonalds
Cardi x Offset x McD dropping 2.14
♬ Cardi B x Offset x McD – McDonald’s
In the same manner, the company partnered with famous R&B singer Swatee to create a custom meal called the Swatee Meal. In addition to promoting the special menu items, Swatee also takes McDonald’s social media accounts for the video series
Swatee Meal Class
:
@mcdonalds
ready class? @officialsaweetie shows u one way to eat the Saweetie Meal ❄️
#mcd
#mcdonalds
#saweetie
#thesaweetiemeal
♬ original sound – McDonald’s
Taking Advantage of Storytelling & Humor
No need to say but… McDonald’s always uses humor and storytelling in its
restaurant marketing campaigns
.
From meme marketing to happy TikTok videos; from video series about a group of friends who love to eat at the restaurant to online competitions, the brand constantly shares funny and relatable content to create a positive association among followers.
Another one?
As an example of that approach, recently, the company started a competition called “Wrap Battle” in TikTok, showing the entertaining moments of employees:
@mcdonalds
who do u think the real winner is?
#competition
#bts
#wrapbattle
#mcd
#mcdonalds
♬ original sound – McDonald’s
Similarly, back in 2022, the marketing team launched a digital marketing campaign called “The McD Challenge.” The campaign challenged customers to create & share their unique meals on social media.
The campaign was a success, generating over 100 million views and over 1 million pieces of content.
Creative Comebacks from Pop Culture
Let’s accept that we remember times when we see McDonald’s and its signature menus everywhere; in series, films, magazines, and even sports events.
The brand, which has a long history of using mass culture references, has focused its digital marketing efforts on creating creative comebacks from pop culture. And, yes, this strategy has been very successful for the company, especially in generating buzz online.
In 2023, the food chain started a “
Wednesday
” dance challenge on TikTok. The ad featured a group of McDonald’s employees doing the dance challenge.  The ad was a fun and engaging way to connect with the target audience while raising brand awareness one more time.
@mcdonaldsm_
dance time for ocheas last shift
#dance
#ochealastday
#work
#mcdonalds
#breakfast
♬ L$d – Luclover
The company also uses pop culture references to promote its latest menu items. When launching its new “Create Your Taste” menu in 2023, the company released an ad that referenced the popular TV show “Chopped” and used the catchphrase “It’s all up to you.”
After that, a great number of people run into the restaurant to make their unique menus, like “making the biggest burger possible” – and have shared their experience with create your taste kiosks.
McDonald’s versus Burger King Marketing Strategy
Both McDonald’s and Burger King
serve burgers and fries
, but when it comes to marketing, they live in entirely different worlds.
One leans on legacy, consistency, and emotional warmth.
And the other’s focus is on bold moves, big stunts, and playful punches—even at its biggest rival.
McDonald’s: The Consistent Crowd-Pleaser
McDonald’s is the world’s comfort zone. It doesn’t shout; it doesn’t shock. Instead, it shows up—reliably and familiarly. Its
“I’m Lovin’ It”
campaign has been going strong for over two decades, evolving subtly to stay culturally relevant while keeping its tone positive and universal.
Take the
“Famous Orders”
campaign. Rather than creating something new, McDonald’s tapped into everyday fan behavior—asking,
what do celebrities order when they go to McDonald’s?
The answers came in the form of special meal bundles featuring Kim Kardashian, Kanye West, Dracula, The Invisible Man, and more!
It was smart, subtle, and wildly successful without breaking character.
Burger King: The Rebel with a Flame-Grilled Cause
Then there’s
Burger King’s marketing strategy
. The brand loves to stir the pot, and its tone is cheeky, unfiltered, and often aimed straight at McDonald’s.
The
“Whopper Detour”
campaign, for instance, offered users a Whopper for 1¢—
but only if they ordered it while standing near a McDonald’s
. Pure mischief, powered by clever geo-targeting.
Two Product Strategies, Two Philosophies
McDonald’s keeps its menu tight and tuned to local tastes. From
McAloo Tikkis in India
to
Teriyaki Burgers in Japan
, the chain proves it knows when to globalize and when to localize. Seasonal items like the
Shamrock Shake
or the return of the
McRib
keep things exciting without shaking the foundation.
Image source:
Wikipedia
Burger King takes the opposite approach, loud and proud about its flame-grilled offerings. It constantly puts the
Whopper
front and center, often calling it the superior burger.
When It Comes to Digital, It’s Brains vs. Bravado
McDonald’s plays the long game in digital. Its app integrates ordering, rewards, and delivery in one clean ecosystem. The
MyMcDonald’s Rewards Program
is a perfect example of value-driven loyalty, using personalization and convenience to keep customers coming back.
Burger King, meanwhile, treats digital as a playground. In the
“Stevenage Challenge,”
it sponsored a tiny football club just to get its logo into FIFA games. Fans were rewarded for scoring goals with Burger King-branded players—and they did, in droves. I believe that it was genius.
So, I  can say that McDonald’s wins hearts through consistency, comfort, and emotional resonance. Burger King wins attention by challenging the norm, embracing chaos, and roasting the competition. Both approaches work, but they’re playing very different games.
McDonald’s Advertising: Culture-Driven, Visual, and Adaptable
McDonald’s keeps its advertising sharp by focusing on
cultural timing and clear, visual ideas
.
Instead of relying on heavy messaging, the brand often builds campaigns around
familiar products and public figures
, making the experience feel
relatable
rather than promotional.
One example featured celebrity meal combinations tied to artists like Kanye West and famous figures like Dracuşa. The strategy didn’t involve launching new items. Instead, it gave existing ones renewed relevance through pop culture alignment. And the simplicity of the campaign made it
easy to understand and easy to share
.
Visual storytelling is another strength.
As marketer
Nehal Kazim
highlighted in a recent LinkedIn breakdown, a McDonald’s ad showing
phones styled like fries
works because it “lands in under two seconds.”
No headline, no slogan—just a clear visual metaphor that speaks on its own:
Emotional visuals
over functional messaging
Sharp concept
instead of layered complexity
Well-timed placement
rooted in cultural context
McDonald’s also allows room for audience participation. In 2023, the Grimace Shake became a viral trend through user-generated TikTok content.
Rather than steering away from the unexpected tone, the brand embraced it, which kept the product in the spotlight far longer than a traditional rollout might have.
Advertising is adjusted by region as well. During the 2024 Paris Olympics, a campaign in France highlighted the brand’s
local nicknames
around the world, turning small cultural details into a message of global unity.
Bonus: How Much Does McDonald’s Spend on Ads?
Although it may seem like the answer to the question is “hundreds of millions of dollars,” the reality is a bit more complex.
The global fast-food giant, McDonald’s, splits its advertising costs into two main categories: advertising cooperatives and production costs for television and radio. According to figures released by Statista, back in 2022, McDonald’s spent
$334.5 million
on advertising cooperatives and
$63.8 million
on the production of radio and TV commercials.
The same year, marketers may remember, the Wall Street Journal announced that McDonald’s was making a significant investment in its marketing strategy by
doubling its advertising budget
.
Its advertising spending will increase to 10% from 4% between 2021 and 2024 to platforms owned by groups including Black, Hispanic and Asian Pacific American people, women and LGBT people. For Black-owned properties, spending will rise to 5% from 2%.
This move, of course, highlights a clear focus on expanding their media reach and potentially signals a shift in how they target consumers. So, there is no exact figure for McDonald’s current advertising budget is elusive,  the news of doubling their budget suggests a significant increase, potentially reaching well over $700 million based on past figures.
What to Remember from McDonald’s Marketing
A box of fries that looks like phone icons.
A celebrity meal that feels like a shared secret.
A jingle that hasn’t changed in decades, but still hits.
McDonald’s focuses on refinement—
on making familiar things feel current
, again and again.
Here’s what to take away:
Culture over noise.
McDonald’s doesn’t shout—it shows up in your feed, in your neighborhood, in your language.
Familiar > flashy.
You already know the menu. But it still feels fresh, because they make it feel like
yours
.
Tech that fits.
Whether it’s mobile ordering or AI-driven personalization, innovation is there, but never in your face.
Localization at its finest.
It’s a global giant, but somehow, it still feels like the corner spot.
Visuals that land.
No words needed—just fries shaped like phones, and your brain fills in the rest.
🧐 Frequently Asked Questions About McDonald’s Marketing Strategy
What marketing strategies does McDonald’s use?
McDonald’s marketing is built around a simple idea: be consistent, feel local, and stay emotionally relevant. The brand leans on its global 7Ps framework while adapting to each market with local menus, cultural references, and community-driven campaigns. Whether it’s a Happy Meal or a pop-up moment with a celebrity, every move is designed to feel familiar and intentional.
What does McDonald’s digital marketing strategy consist of?
McDonald’s digital game is strong because it knows how to blend storytelling with utility. From app-based loyalty programs to trend-savvy TikToks, the brand uses humor, nostalgia, and real-time engagement to keep its voice fresh. Its content isn’t just promotional — it’s made to be part of how people already communicate online.
How does McDonald’s promote their products?
Promotion at McDonald’s is more about moments than messages. Sometimes it’s a minimalist billboard that doubles as wayfinding. Other times, it’s a limited-edition meal built around pop culture. The brand uses simple visuals, recognizable cues, and sharp timing — all grounded in the idea that the familiar can still surprise you.
How does McDonald’s use social media?
McDonald’s doesn’t treat social media like a checklist — it uses each platform in its own voice. TikTok becomes a space for user challenges and celebrity takeovers, while Instagram leans into bold visuals and nostalgic throwbacks. Campaigns are built to be shared, not just seen, and that’s what keeps the brand part of the conversation.
Who is McDonald’s target audience?
The short answer: almost everyone. But McDonald’s doesn’t speak to the crowd in one voice. It creates tailored messages based on data — age, habits, location, lifestyle. A parent might see a Happy Meal ad. A student might scroll past a meme about fries at midnight. The goal is always the same: meet people where they are, in a way that feels personal.
🫵 Got thoughts on McDonald’s marketing?
Share this on LinkedIn and tag us—or me! I’d love to chat.
And, if you are hungry for more brand analysis and real marketing insights, you should check out the other marketing strategy deep dives of famous brands below.
Share this post

I'm a lover of art, cinema, literature, and design. And as a proud queer activist, I strive to create a more inclusive world. Join me on my journey; connect with me on LinkedIn!

pages.title: McDonald's Canada Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine 2026 | Cossette Campaign
pages.content:
Home

»
McDonald’s Canada Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine Returns in 2026: Inside the Cossette “For Fans. From Fans.” Pre-Playoff Campaign
February 25, 2026
9 mins read
Business
McDonald’s Canada Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine Returns in 2026: Inside the Cossette “For Fans. From Fans.” Pre-Playoff Campaign
By
ALM Corp
When McDonald’s Canada brought back the Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine in February 2026, the decision was not driven by a quiet menu refresh. It was a strategically timed move anchored to the Toronto Raptors’ late-season push and built on a campaign idea that positions comfort food as a shared act of fandom. Developed by
Cossette
, McDonald’s Canada’s long-standing creative agency of record, the campaign runs under the line
“For Fans. From Fans.”
— and every element of the execution, from the custom packaging to the mascot-led launch spot, is designed to operate inside Raptors culture rather than beside it.
This post breaks down exactly what the campaign is, how it was built, why it works, what the broader marketing strategy tells us about McDonald’s Canada’s approach to sports partnerships, and what marketers and brand strategists can take away from it.
What Is the Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine?
The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine is a limited-time menu item available exclusively at participating McDonald’s locations in Ontario. The product builds on the McDonald’s Canada poutine platform — a permanent fixture on the Canadian menu that already includes the classic base of World Famous Fries™, cheese curds, and savoury gravy — but elevates it with Raptors-specific flavour additions.
The full build includes:
World Famous Fries™
as the base
Cheese curds
— a nod to the classically Canadian poutine format
Savoury gravy
layered throughout
100% Canadian-raised seasoned chicken
, emphasising the domestic sourcing commitment McDonald’s Canada has made a signature of its product communications
Tortilla strips
for crunch and nacho-inspired texture
Nacho cheese sauce
drizzled on top
The combination takes a familiar Canadian comfort food and adds flavour layering that connects it visually and texturally to game-day snacking. It is the kind of food you eat while watching sport — salty, shareable, and satisfying — and the product design reflects that intent precisely.
The item returns in
custom Raptors packaging
, which plays a significant role in the brand identity strategy. The packaging is not merely branded; it visually connects the food product to the team’s identity at the point of purchase, making it an artefact of fandom rather than a standard fast-food container.
The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine is available for a limited time only. The “final buzzer” framing — used in McDonald’s own communications at the item’s original launch — is not accidental. It applies playoff urgency to the act of buying a side dish.
Source
The Campaign: “For Fans. From Fans.”
The creative brief behind this campaign, as articulated by Cossette, centres on a single behavioural truth: the pre-playoff period generates a distinct and heightened form of fan engagement. It is not the same as midseason fandom. It carries urgency, it carries collective optimism, and it comes with rituals — the food people eat, the places they gather, the ways they signal their allegiance.
Andrew Chisholm, Executive Creative Director at Cossette, framed the intent clearly:
“Pre-playoffs fandom comes with a different kind of intensity. We wanted the work to feel like it was coming from inside Raptors culture, reflecting how fans actually experience game day, where McDonald’s is part of the ritual and shared favourites carry real meaning.”
Source
That phrase — “coming from inside Raptors culture” — is the key strategic signal. The campaign is not positioned as a sponsorship announcement or a promotional push. It is positioned as a genuine fan statement. The tagline “For Fans. From Fans.” operationalises this: it implies that McDonald’s Canada and Cossette are not outsiders inserting a product into fandom, but participants in it.
Michael Curds, Ontario Marketing Manager at McDonald’s Canada, reinforced this framing:
“The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine has always had a strong connection with fans. As fans lock in to support the team’s playoff push, bringing it back felt like a natural way to show that we’re fans too and to be part of those game-day moments.”
Source
The word “too” in that statement carries real strategic weight. McDonald’s Canada is not positioning itself as a sponsor or a vendor at the party. It is positioning itself as a fan — one that happens to have a poutine to bring along.
Cossette’s Creative Execution: The Mascot as Cultural Anchor
The launch spot centres on the Toronto Raptors mascot — known as the Raptor — emerging from a McDonald’s paper bag to reveal the poutine. This is not a coincidence of casting. The Raptor mascot is one of the most recognisable characters in Canadian sports culture and carries a kind of irreverent, crowd-oriented energy that is native to the Scotiabank Arena atmosphere.
The spot uses close-ups of the product paired with a fan taking a bite in front of a packed arena — a deliberate visual choice that frames the poutine not as a restaurant item but as something that belongs in the context of live basketball. The LBB report notes that the Raptors’ mascot is positioned as the hero behind the comeback, reviving the ‘Courtside Legend’ campaign character.
Source
The campaign extends across social channels and digital placements, with in-arena activations timed to the team’s pre-playoff schedule. The integrated rollout includes TV, online video, out-of-home, and in-arena elements, ensuring the message reaches fans at every touchpoint where they consume Raptors content — whether that is at Scotiabank Arena, on their phone, or watching a broadcast at home.
By celebrating fandom at every level — from McDonald’s crew members to McDelivery drivers to fans watching from home — the campaign positions McDonald’s Canada not as a corporate partner standing at the edge of basketball culture, but as a participant in it. The McDelivery driver inclusion is particularly astute: it acknowledges that a significant portion of game-day food consumption now happens in living rooms, not arena concessions.
McDonald’s Canada and the Toronto Raptors: A Partnership Eight Years in the Making
This campaign does not exist in isolation. McDonald’s Canada has been a proud partner of the Toronto Raptors since 2018, according to official McDonald’s Canada communications. That partnership has deepened with each season, producing a catalogue of integrations that span product launches, community initiatives, digital promotions, and cultural moments.
The most consequential early chapter of the partnership came in the 2018-19 NBA season, when McDonald’s Canada promised free medium fries to Ontario customers when the Raptors scored a certain number of three-pointers in a game. The promotion was designed for a mid-table team. It was not designed for the team that would go on to win the 2019 NBA Championship. The Raptors’ historic run, as covered by the Financial Post at the time, left McDonald’s Canada on the hook for millions in free French fries — a marketing outcome that became a lesson in both the risks and the rewards of tying brand promotions to live sports performance.
Source
Notably, McDonald’s Canada reportedly chose the Raptors over the Toronto Maple Leafs as their primary sports partner because Raptors fans were more aligned with McDonald’s target demographic. That is a data-driven partnership decision — not a prestige play.
The relationship matured through the
Beyond The Arch
programme, which links Raptors three-point scores to exclusive McDonald’s app offers. This season’s edition, tied to the Raptors’ 30th anniversary, set the trigger at 10 three-pointers per game to earn an exclusive offer in the McDonald’s app the following day. The promotional architecture drives app downloads, increases digital engagement, and ties McDonald’s commercial outcomes directly to in-game performance.
The
Power of 3s
initiative, now in its third season, adds a community dimension to the same mechanics. For every regular-season three-pointer scored by a Toronto Raptor, a youth athlete in the Ontario Basketball League (U14 and up), a fan playing the Beyond The Arch mobile game on the Raptors app, or a community member at local events, McDonald’s Canada donates $133 to MLSE Foundation’s Change the Game programme, with a goal of raising $200,000 per season.
Source
This is sports marketing operating at three concurrent levels simultaneously: commercial promotion, digital engagement, and community investment.
Most recently, in February 2025, McDonald’s Canada launched the
’95 McFlurry
— a soft-serve vanilla ice cream dessert featuring blueberry-flavoured syrup and red pop candy, a deliberate reference to the original 1995 Raptors team colours. The McFlurry and the Toronto Raptors both debuted in 1995, and Cossette built an entire music campaign around that shared origin, including a three-song EP produced in a ’90s hip-hop style and developed in partnership with California-based Beacon Street Studios. The campaign included connected TV, digital and social elements, as well as activations at Scotiabank Arena.
Source
The 2026 Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine campaign sits inside this established relationship. It is the latest chapter in a partnership that has, over eight seasons, moved from promotional giveaways to product launches to cultural storytelling.
Why Ontario? The Strategic Logic of Regional Focus
The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine is available exclusively in Ontario. That is not a logistical limitation — it is a deliberate strategic choice.
The Toronto Raptors are Ontario’s team. While they carry national Canadian significance — there is no other Canadian NBA franchise — the intensity of fandom is naturally concentrated in Ontario, and particularly in the Greater Toronto Area. A national rollout of a Raptors-branded product would dilute the local relevance and scatter the marketing energy across a geography where the emotional connection is weaker.
By confining the product to Ontario, McDonald’s Canada creates a
scarcity effect at a geographic scale
. Ontarians can access the Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine. Canadians elsewhere cannot. That exclusivity reinforces the product’s connection to Raptors identity — it is not just a limited-time item, it is a regional badge of fandom.
Regional focus also allows the campaign’s digital and out-of-home placements to be tightly concentrated in markets where the advertising has the highest contextual resonance. A billboard referencing Raptors playoff energy in downtown Toronto lands differently than the same message in Vancouver. Concentration of media weight in high-relevance markets increases the effective reach of each advertising dollar.
Source
The Psychology of Limited-Time Offers in Sports-Tied Marketing
The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine is a Limited-Time Offer (LTO) timed to a specific competitive window — the pre-playoff stretch of the NBA regular season. Understanding why this works requires looking at both the psychology of LTOs and the psychology of sports fandom.
Data from Technomic indicates that LTO launches are up 19% year over year, with nearly 4,000 new LTO items introduced in November 2025 alone, according to Restaurant Business Online. The market’s appetite for limited-time items has not diminished — it has accelerated.
Source
According to The Food Institute, citing Restobiz research, 91% of people report being more inclined to visit a restaurant that has new items on its menu. Technomic’s Lizzy Freier notes that approximately 40% of consumers say they are more likely to try new and unique flavours than they were three years ago — and a third are willing to pay more for a restaurant meal that features those flavours.
Source
Reilly Newman, founder at Motif Brands, frames the mechanism as FOMO — Fear of Missing Out. An LTO creates scarcity where none would otherwise exist in a fast-food context. Access to supply is not the constraint; time is. McDonald’s, with the McDonald’s Canada app, has a direct channel to communicate that urgency to registered users without media spend.
Michael Della Penna, InMarket’s Chief Strategy Officer, notes that LTOs can engage new diners while keeping loyal customers through product innovation. The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine serves both functions: it gives Raptors fans who might not regularly visit McDonald’s a reason to walk in, while giving regular McDonald’s customers a reason to feel that this particular visit is special.
The sports-timing dimension adds a second layer. The pre-playoff period is a window of heightened emotional engagement among sports fans. Research from GWI indicates that among the 71.7 million American sports fans, 36.3 million are regular fast-food customers. The overlap between sports fandom and QSR consumption is significant, and the emotional state that fans bring to that window — the anticipation, the anxiety, the collective energy — is commercially valuable.
Source
By launching an LTO in the pre-playoff window specifically, McDonald’s Canada is embedding its product into the emotional architecture of fandom at the moment when that architecture is most active.
Poutine as a Canadian Cultural Symbol — And Why That Matters for the Brand
Poutine is not simply a menu item in Canada. It is a cultural signifier. Originating in Quebec, it has expanded to become a dish that Canadians claim as their own — a fusion of simplicity, comfort, and national identity. Its presence on the McDonald’s Canada menu reflects an understanding that Canadian consumers expect Canadian brands, even American-owned global franchises, to speak to Canadian food culture authentically.
The decision to base the Raptors partnership product on poutine rather than a burger or a sandwich is deliberate. Poutine is what you eat when you are home, gathered with people you know, watching something that matters. It carries the same emotional positioning the campaign is built around. A Raptors-branded burger would have been a promotion. A Raptors-branded poutine is a cultural statement.
The nacho twist — the tortilla strips and nacho cheese sauce — adds a game-day snacking dimension to a traditionally sit-down comfort food. It creates a product that is specifically adapted for game-night consumption: shareable, layered with multiple flavours, and requiring no formal dining context. The name itself — “Chicken Nacho Poutine” — communicates exactly what the experience is.
La Poutine Week, described as Canada’s biggest poutine festival, reflects the extent to which poutine has become a platform for creativity and brand expression in Canadian food culture. McDonald’s Canada has used that cultural platform wisely by adding its own chapter each basketball season.
Source
Cossette’s Broader Track Record with McDonald’s Canada
This campaign did not emerge from a new creative relationship. Cossette has been McDonald’s Canada’s creative agency of record for an extended period, and the breadth of their collaboration is visible across the brand’s most culturally visible campaigns.
At the 2025 Effie Awards Canada, McDonald’s Canada was the most-listed advertiser, earning eight nominations across three campaigns created by Cossette. That is a significant performance indicator — Effie Awards measure effectiveness, not just craft, which means the McDonald’s Canada/Cossette work is evaluated as campaigns that produced measurable business results, not just admirable creative work.
Source
Other notable Cossette/McDonald’s Canada campaigns include:
The Showdown
(early 2025): A campaign featuring NHL superstars Connor McDavid and Auston Matthews, paying homage to the McDonald’s hockey commercials of the ’90s. Like the Raptors work, the campaign built on nostalgia while operating within a specific Canadian sports culture.
The International Menu Heist
: A cinematic 360° campaign built like a modern heist film, promoting six global menu favourites added to McDonald’s Canada’s menu. The campaign demonstrated Cossette’s range — genre filmmaking applied to fast food.
The $5 McValue Meal
: A national, music-driven campaign designed to reposition value messaging without reducing it to price-point advertising.
The ’95 McFlurry
(February 2025): The Raptors 30th anniversary campaign, built around a three-song ’90s hip-hop EP and featuring the original 1995 Raptors colours in a soft-serve product. ECD Jason Hill noted that the hip-hop direction emerged from the long-standing connection between basketball culture and hip-hop music.
Source
Each of these campaigns shares a consistent creative signature: they locate a genuine cultural truth — nostalgia, sports fandom, national food identity — and build a product experience around it rather than beside it. The 2026 Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine campaign follows the same formula: identify the moment (pre-playoffs), identify the cultural truth (fans have rituals), and build the product into that ritual.
What Brands and Marketers Can Take Away From This Campaign
The McDonald’s Canada/Cossette/Raptors collaboration offers a set of practical observations for any brand considering a sports partnership or a culturally embedded limited-time offer strategy.
Timing is a media strategy in itself.
The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine’s return is timed to the pre-playoff period specifically. Not the start of the season. Not the All-Star break. The pre-playoff window — when fan engagement peaks before the final stretch of meaningful games. This timing means that even without increasing media weight, the campaign lands with greater urgency and relevance simply because of when it appears.
Packaging can function as earned media.
Custom Raptors packaging is not just a brand-building exercise — it is a shareable object. When fans receive their poutine in a Raptors box, they are more likely to photograph it, share it, and post it. The packaging becomes a piece of fan content without any additional media spend.
Regional restriction can amplify relevance.
Limiting the product to Ontario creates scarcity, exclusivity, and a local identity signal that a national rollout would dissolve. The principle applies to any brand considering how geographic distribution affects perceived value.
Mascot characters carry cultural memory.
The Raptors mascot is not just a costume character — it is a recurring figure in thousands of Ontarians’ lived experiences of basketball. Using the mascot as the creative hero of the launch activates that cultural memory and lowers the cognitive distance between the product and the fan experience.
Community investment deepens commercial partnerships.
The Power of 3s initiative, which donates to MLSE Foundation’s Change the Game on the basis of three-pointer activity, converts the McDonald’s/Raptors partnership from a commercial arrangement into something with community stakes. Fans who care about youth basketball in Ontario now have a reason to cheer for three-pointers that goes beyond the game. That emotional layering strengthens the brand relationship.
LTOs work best when they are tied to something fans already care about.
The poutine is not creating demand by itself. It is attaching itself to demand that already exists — playoff fandom, game-day rituals, Raptors identity. The product becomes a participant in an event, rather than an event unto itself.
As noted by DesignRush in their analysis of the campaign: “Anchoring limited-time products in existing fan rituals can accelerate cultural adoption. Regional focus can intensify cultural relevance faster than national scale. Packaging and creative that reflect audience identity can strengthen emotional engagement. Timing releases to competitive milestones can increase urgency without increasing media weight.”
Source
McDonald’s Canada’s Broader Sports Marketing Playbook in 2026
The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine campaign does not exist in isolation from McDonald’s Canada’s broader sports marketing activity. In February 2026, the brand also launched a collaboration with Toronto-based rapper Drake — the “Afters Meal” — which operates in a similar cultural space: Toronto identity, music, and community. The timing of both the Drake collaboration and the Raptors poutine within weeks of each other suggests a deliberate concentration of culturally resonant Toronto-specific marketing activity in the early months of 2026.
Earlier in the season, the Showdown campaign had already positioned McDonald’s Canada within hockey culture through Connor McDavid and Auston Matthews — two of the sport’s most prominent figures — in a campaign that used nostalgia to do contemporary brand work.
Taken together, these activations suggest that McDonald’s Canada’s sports marketing strategy in 2026 follows a consistent internal logic: identify the most culturally significant sports moments in the Canadian calendar, build product-led campaigns that operate inside those moments rather than alongside them, and use the brand’s existing cultural equity (nostalgia, community investment, fan identity) to deepen relevance at each touchpoint.
Cossette, as the agency orchestrating these campaigns, has developed a deep understanding of how to move between different Canadian sports cultures — basketball, hockey, hip-hop, food — with campaigns that feel native to each one rather than imported into them.
Frequently Asked Questions
What is the McDonald’s Canada Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine?
The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine is a limited-time menu item available at participating McDonald’s locations in Ontario, Canada. It is built on a base of McDonald’s World Famous Fries™, layered with cheese curds and savoury gravy, topped with 100% Canadian-raised seasoned chicken, tortilla strips, and drizzled with nacho cheese sauce. The product comes in custom Raptors-branded packaging created exclusively for the promotion.
When did the Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine return in 2026?
The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine returned in February 2026, timed to the NBA pre-playoff period as the Toronto Raptors built late-season momentum ahead of the post-season. The campaign was announced on February 19, 2026, per DesignRush.
Is the Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine available across Canada?
No. The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine is available exclusively in Ontario at participating McDonald’s restaurants. This regional focus is a deliberate marketing strategy that aligns the product with Toronto Raptors fandom, which is most concentrated in Ontario.
What agency created the 2026 Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine campaign?
The campaign was developed by
Cossette
, McDonald’s Canada’s creative agency of record. Andrew Chisholm, Executive Creative Director at Cossette, led the creative development for the 2026 campaign. Cossette has a long-standing relationship with McDonald’s Canada, with the brand earning eight Effie Award nominations at the 2025 Effie Awards Canada for three Cossette-created campaigns.
What is the campaign tagline for the 2026 Raptors poutine campaign?
The campaign runs under the line
“For Fans. From Fans.”
This tagline positions McDonald’s Canada as a fellow Raptors fan rather than a corporate sponsor, embedding the brand inside the fan experience rather than above it.
What does the Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine launch ad feature?
The launch spot features the Toronto Raptors mascot — known as the Raptor — emerging from a McDonald’s paper bag to reveal the poutine. The creative revives the ‘Courtside Legend’ character and pairs product close-ups with shots of a fan eating the poutine in front of a packed arena.
How long has McDonald’s Canada been a partner of the Toronto Raptors?
McDonald’s Canada has been a proud partner of the Toronto Raptors since 2018. The partnership has produced a range of activations including the Beyond The Arch promotional programme, the Power of 3s community initiative, the ’95 McFlurry in 2025, and the Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine. McDonald’s Canada reportedly chose the Raptors over the Toronto Maple Leafs as their primary sports partner because Raptors fans were more closely aligned with McDonald’s core demographic.
What is the “Beyond The Arch” programme?
Beyond The Arch is a McDonald’s Canada promotional programme that links Toronto Raptors three-point scores to exclusive offers for McDonald’s app users. In the current season, when the Raptors score 10 or more three-pointers in a game, fans can redeem an exclusive offer in the McDonald’s app the following day. The programme drives app downloads, increases digital engagement, and ties McDonald’s commercial outcomes directly to Raptors gameplay.
What is the Power of 3s initiative?
The Power of 3s is a community investment programme that has been running for three seasons. For every three-pointer scored during the regular season — by a Toronto Raptor, a youth athlete in the Ontario Basketball League (U14 and up), a fan on the Beyond The Arch mobile game, or a community member at local events — McDonald’s Canada donates $133 to MLSE Foundation’s Change the Game, with a fundraising goal of $200,000 per season.
What was the ’95 McFlurry?
The ’95 McFlurry was a limited-time product launched in February 2025 by McDonald’s Canada to celebrate the 30th anniversary of both the Toronto Raptors and the McFlurry dessert, both of which debuted in 1995. The McFlurry featured soft-serve vanilla ice cream, blueberry-flavoured syrup, and red pop candy — a reference to the original 1995 Raptors colours of purple and red. The campaign, also created by Cossette, included a three-song ’90s hip-hop EP and activations at Scotiabank Arena.
Why does McDonald’s Canada use poutine for its Raptors partnership?
Poutine holds cultural significance in Canada that extends well beyond its status as a menu item. It is a nationally claimed comfort food with Quebec origins that has become a symbol of Canadian food identity. McDonald’s Canada’s decision to build its Raptors partnership product around poutine — rather than a burger or a sandwich — positions the collaboration within genuinely Canadian food culture. Poutine is also contextually appropriate for game-day eating: shareable, hearty, and suited to casual group settings.
How does Cossette approach McDonald’s Canada sports campaigns?
Based on the available campaign record, Cossette’s approach to McDonald’s Canada sports campaigns follows a consistent pattern: identify a genuine cultural truth connected to a specific Canadian sports moment, build a product or experience that feels native to that culture rather than inserted into it, and use timing — the specific window in the sports calendar — as a media strategy in its own right. The ’95 McFlurry used the 30th anniversary and ’90s nostalgia. The Showdown used hockey nostalgia. The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine uses pre-playoff fan ritual. Each campaign starts with a human truth rather than a product feature.
What does “For Fans. From Fans.” mean as a brand positioning?
The tagline is a stance declaration. It communicates that McDonald’s Canada is not approaching the Raptors partnership from a corporate distance but from a position of genuine fandom. By claiming “From Fans,” the brand places itself alongside the consumers it is speaking to rather than above them. The positioning is consistent with the campaign’s broader strategy of making McDonald’s feel embedded in Raptors culture rather than adjacent to it.
Why are limited-time offers effective for fast-food sports partnerships?
Limited-time offers (LTOs) create scarcity in a context where supply is otherwise unlimited. In a fast-food environment, you can visit McDonald’s any day — but you can only order the Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine for a few weeks. That constraint creates urgency. When the LTO is tied to a sports window — a team’s playoff push — the urgency is doubled: the team’s season has its own expiry date, and the two timelines reinforce each other. Research shows that 91% of consumers are more likely to visit a restaurant with new menu items, and 40% are more likely to try new and unique flavours than they were three years ago, according to Technomic data cited by The Food Institute.
What role does the Raptors mascot play in the campaign?
The Raptors mascot — known as the Raptor — serves as the creative hero of the launch spot, reviving the character as the “Courtside Legend.” The mascot is one of the most recognisable characters in Canadian sports culture, carrying cultural memory from thousands of fans’ live game experiences. Using the mascot as the face of the product launch activates that cultural familiarity and creates an immediate visual shorthand connecting the food product to the Raptors fan experience.
Where does the Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine campaign appear?
The campaign runs across television (including connected TV), online video, social media, out-of-home (OOH) placements, and in-arena activations at Scotiabank Arena throughout February and March 2026. The integrated rollout ensures the campaign reaches Raptors fans across every platform where they consume basketball content.
How does this campaign compare to McDonald’s Drake “Afters Meal” collaboration?
Both the Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine campaign and the “Afters Meal” collaboration with Drake launched in early 2026 and reflect McDonald’s Canada’s strategy of concentrating culturally resonant Toronto-specific marketing activities within a tight window. While the Drake collaboration operates within music and nightlife culture, the Raptors poutine campaign operates within sports and game-day culture. Together they represent different facets of Toronto identity — basketball, hip-hop, comfort food — that McDonald’s Canada is using to build cultural relevance simultaneously across distinct audience segments.
Can I order the Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine through McDelivery?
The campaign specifically acknowledges McDelivery as part of the game-day ritual, with McDonald’s delivery drivers included in the creative’s celebration of fandom at every level. The poutine is available through the McDonald’s app and McDelivery for fans watching games at home, not just those eating in-restaurant.
What makes this campaign different from a standard sports sponsorship?
A standard sports sponsorship typically involves logo placement, arena signage, and broadcast mentions. The “For Fans. From Fans.” campaign operates differently: it produces original creative content, a culturally relevant product, custom packaging, and a community investment component. The brand does not just appear within the Raptors experience — it has built a product, a piece of content, and a community initiative that are each independently meaningful to fans. That depth of integration distinguishes the campaign from traditional sponsorship mechanics.
How does McDonald’s Canada use the app in connection with its Raptors partnership?
The McDonald’s app is central to the Beyond The Arch promotional mechanic, which distributes exclusive offers to users the day after the Raptors score 10 or more three-pointers in a game. The app is also used to distribute promotions tied to McDonald’s Canada’s in-arena activations and its broader loyalty programme. The Raptors partnership effectively functions as a repeating, game-driven reason for fans to download and open the McDonald’s app throughout the season.
What This Campaign Tells Us About the Future of Sports-Embedded Brand Marketing
The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine campaign, in its 2026 iteration, reflects a maturation of the McDonald’s Canada/Cossette sports marketing approach. The brand has moved well beyond simple sponsorship — logo at the arena, banner in the broadcast — into territory where the partnership produces original products, original content, community outcomes, and digital engagement mechanics that run across the full length of a season.
The “For Fans. From Fans.” line is not just a tagline. It is a positioning that asks the brand to earn its place in the conversation rather than buy it. McDonald’s Canada earns it through product relevance (a poutine that fits game-day eating), creative authenticity (a campaign built from inside fan culture), cultural specificity (Ontario exclusivity, Canadian chicken sourcing, poutine as national food identity), and community investment (the Power of 3s programme).
The lesson for brands operating in sports partnership contexts is that the pre-playoff window is not only a media planning consideration — it is an emotional one. Fans in that window are already primed for intensity, community, and shared experience. A brand that positions its product as part of that ritual does not need to manufacture emotion. It only needs to show up at the right moment, with the right thing, in the right way.
That is what McDonald’s Canada and Cossette have done with the return of the Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine in 2026. The product is back for a limited time. The season is ending. The intensity is building. And a paper bag full of cheesy, gravy-covered fries and seasoned chicken has become, for Raptors fans in Ontario, as much a part of the pre-playoff ritual as watching the starting lineup warm up.
About ALM Corp
ALM Corp
is a full-service digital marketing agency with over 20 years of experience delivering integrated strategies across SEO, content marketing, paid advertising, branding, and social media for businesses and agencies worldwide. With more than 30,000 satisfied clients and a proven track record of increasing leads by up to 400%, improving CTR by 200%, and driving measurable revenue growth, ALM Corp brings the same cultural intelligence and strategic rigour to its clients’ campaigns that McDonald’s Canada and Cossette bring to theirs.
The kind of sports-embedded, culturally timed campaign examined in this post — built on audience insight, precise timing, regional relevance, and LTO mechanics — represents exactly the type of integrated thinking ALM Corp applies to digital campaign strategy. Whether you are a brand looking to build cultural relevance through content and search, or an agency looking for a white-label digital marketing partner capable of crafting and executing high-performance campaigns, ALM Corp has the tools, the team, and the track record to help you compete at that level.
The Raptor Chicken Nacho Poutine works because McDonald’s Canada and Cossette know their audience, understand the moment, and build every element of the campaign to serve the fan experience. Great digital marketing works the same way: it starts with knowing who you are talking to, when they are ready to listen, and what they actually care about. That is what ALM Corp does — for agencies and businesses across Canada and globally.
